statement.markdown: There are N stones. The height of each stone is given in an array heights. A frog is initially at the first stone (index 0) and wants to reach the last stone (index N-1). From stone i, the frog can jump to any stone j where i + 1 <= j <= min(i + K, N - 1). The cost of jumping from stone i to stone j is |heights[i] - heights[j]|. Find the minimum cost to reach the last stone.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers N and K, representing the number of stones and the maximum jump distance respectively.
The second line contains N space-separated integers representing the heights of the stones.
statement.outputFormat: A single integer representing the minimum cost to reach the last stone.

hints.text: The problem can be approached using a graph traversal algorithm, where each stone is a node and two nodes are connected if the absolute difference of their positions is in K.

The frog can only jump to stones that are within the allowed jump distances specified in K. This means for any given stone, you only need to consider stones that are at a position which can be reached by adding or subtracting a value in K from the current stone's position.

A breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm can be effectively used to solve this problem. The algorithm should start from the first stone and explore all reachable stones level by level, checking if the last stone can be reached.

editorial.title: Frog Jump with K Distances: A Leap towards Efficiency
editorial.intuition: The problem requires determining if a frog can jump from the first stone to the last stone using given distances. A brute force approach would involve exploring all possible paths, but this is inefficient. A key observation is that we can use a more efficient algorithm by utilizing the fact that the frog can only jump to stones that exist in the set of stone positions.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves using a recursive function with backtracking to explore all possible jumps from each stone, checking if the frog can reach the last stone. This approach is correct but results in an exponential time complexity of O(n * m * 2^m) due to the repeated exploration of the same paths.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient solution can be achieved by using dynamic programming and observing that we only need to consider the reachable stones.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can create a set of stone positions for efficient lookups and use a recursive function with memoization to store the results of subproblems, avoiding redundant calculations.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it explores all possible paths to the last stone without repeating any subproblems. This is achieved through the use of memoization, which stores the results of subproblems and avoids redundant calculations.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution with sample inputs shows that it produces the correct results and has a significant improvement in performance compared to the brute force approach.
commonMistakes: Not using a set for efficient lookups of stone positions
Not memoizing the results of subproblems
Not considering the base case where the frog is already on the last stone
Incorrectly handling the case where the frog jumps to a stone that is not in the set of stone positions
Not using a recursive function with backtracking
Not exploring all possible paths to the last stone

functionSignature.java: public int frogJump(int[] heights, int K);
functionSignature.python: def frogJump(self, heights: list[int], K: int) -> int:
